title: Internal Commands
description: Learn about internal commands and operators in MUDPuppy

sections.title: What Are Internal Commands?
sections.content:
Internal commands are special instructions that MUDPuppy processes before sending to the MUD server. They provide automation features like variables, aliases, conditionals, and timers.

Internal commands start with a special operator character: #, @, $, or %

sections.title: Operator Overview
sections.content:
MUDPuppy recognizes four operator families:

| Operator | Purpose | Example |
|----------|---------|---------|
| # | Structured directives | #IF, #SET, #TIMER |
| @ | Alias references | @attack |
| $ | User variables | $target, ${target} |
| % | System variables | %1, %TIME |

These operators let you create powerful automation scripts.

sections.title: Escape Sequences
sections.content:
If you need to use the operator characters literally (not as commands), prefix them with a backslash:

| Escape | Result |
|--------|--------|
| \# | Literal # character |
| \@ | Literal @ character |
| \$ | Literal $ character |
| \% | Literal % character |

Example: Typing '\#hello' sends '#hello' to the MUD server.

sections.title: User Variables ($)
sections.content:
User variables store values you define for use in aliases and triggers.

**Syntax:** $variablename or ${variablename}

**Creating a variable:**
#SET target=goblin

**Using a variable:**
kill $target
→ kill goblin

Variables persist across sessions and can be edited in the Variables panel.

sections.title: System Variables (%)
sections.content:
System variables provide access to session and client information.

**Numeric form (%1, %2, etc.):**
Session-scoped arguments, cleared on disconnect.
- %1 - First argument
- %2 - Second argument
- And so on...

**Named form (%TIME, %CHARACTER, etc.):**
Read-only client data.
- %TIME - Current time
- %DATE - Current date
- %CHARACTER - Your character name
- %SERVER - MUD server address
- %SESSIONID - Session identifier
- %HOST - Host address
- %PORT - Port number

sections.title: Alias References (@)
sections.content:
Use @ to invoke an alias by name.

**Syntax:** @aliasname

If you have an alias named 'attack' with pattern 'k' and replacement 'kill $target', you can also invoke it directly:

@attack

This is useful in triggers or timers when you want to call an alias by name.

sections.title: Structured Directives (#)
sections.content:
Structured directives control automation behavior:

| Directive | Description |
|-----------|-------------|
| #IF condition | Begin conditional block |
| #ELSE | Alternate condition branch |
| #ENDIF | End conditional block |
| #SET name=value | Assign value to variable |
| #TIMER name=seconds | Create/update timer |
| #START timer | Start a timer |
| #STOP timer | Stop a timer |
| #CHECK timer | Check timer status |
| #CANCEL timer | Delete a timer |

Directives must be typed as complete commands (not in alias patterns).

sections.title: #IF Conditionals
sections.content:
Use #IF to conditionally execute commands:

#IF $target=goblin
  kill $target
  get gold
#ELSE
  say I don't see a goblin here!
#ENDIF

**Comparison operators:**
- = (equals)
- != (not equals)

**Combining conditions:**
#IF $target=goblin AND $weapon=sword
  attack!
#ENDIF

sections.title: #SET Variable Assignment
sections.content:
Use #SET to create or update variables:

#SET target=dragon
#SET heal=potion

**Using in aliases:**
Pattern: 'k'
Replacement: 'kill $target'

Now typing 'k' sends 'kill dragon'.

**Important:** Variables are profile-specific and persist across sessions.

sections.title: #TIMER Timer Commands
sections.content:
Timers execute commands at regular intervals:

**Create a timer:**
#TIMER heal=30

This creates a timer named 'heal' that fires every 30 seconds.

**Timer commands:**
- #START heal - Start the timer
- #STOP heal - Stop the timer  
- #CHECK heal - Check timer status
- #CANCEL heal - Delete the timer

The timer action comes from your configured triggers.

sections.title: Pass-Through Behavior
sections.content:
Most text you type is sent directly to the MUD server:

- Regular commands (look, get sword, say hello) pass through
- Unknown commands with operators return errors
- Undefined variables pass through literally (${undefined} stays as ${undefined})

This ensures compatibility with MUD servers that use similar syntax.

sections.title: Command Processing Order
sections.content:
When you type a command, MUDPuppy processes it in this order:

1. **Input** - You type a command
2. **Alias expansion** - @ references and aliases are expanded
3. **Variable substitution** - $ and % variables are replaced
4. **Logic evaluation** - #IF/#ELSE/#ENDIF blocks are evaluated
5. **Queue dispatch** - Commands are queued for execution
6. **Trigger evaluation** - Triggers monitor output

This deterministic order ensures predictable automation behavior.
